title: Present Perfect

videoQuiz1.text: Focus on Rachel's line: “I was with Josh for an hour today and he has not asked me out.”
Is the “today” period over?
videoQuiz1.explanation: No, it is still today — the period has not ended, which is why we use Present Perfect.

videoQuiz1.text: Is it still possible for Josh to ask Rachel out today?
videoQuiz1.explanation: Yes — because “today” is still ongoing. Present Perfect is used when the time period is not finished yet.

videoQuiz1.text: Does Rachel care about when Josh did not ask her out, or the result right now?
options: When Josh did not ask her out
The result that Josh has not asked her out
videoQuiz1.explanation: Present Perfect focuses on the result in the present, not the exact time in the past.

videoQuiz1.text:
Focus on Rachel's and Phoebe's lines:
“Rachel: I have never asked a guy out before.
Phoebe: You have never asked a guy before?
Rachel: No. Have you?
Phoebe: Yes, thousands of times.”
Is Rachel talking about something that happened once, or an experience of her whole life?
options: An experience of her whole life
videoQuiz1.explanation: Rachel is talking about her whole life — she has never asked a guy out at any point in her entire life. This is a life experience, not a single past event.

videoQuiz1.text: Did Rachel ask a guy out in her life at least once?
videoQuiz1.explanation: “Never” means zero times — Rachel has never done it.

videoQuiz1.text: Is Rachel's life finished? Could she ask a guy out in the future?
videoQuiz1.explanation: Yes — Rachel is still alive, so she could ask a guy out in the future. Present Perfect life experiences are always about a period up to now, not a closed, finished life.

videoQuiz1.text:
Compare these two sentences:
  🔵 A: John did not ask Rachel yesterday.
  🟡 B: John has never asked Rachel out.
Which one is a finished action at a specific time?
videoQuiz1.explanation: A: “John did not ask Rachel yesterday.” — Past Simple is used for finished actions at a specific time (“yesterday”).

videoQuiz1.text: Which one is a life experience?
videoQuiz1.explanation: B: “John has never asked Rachel out.” — Present Perfect with “never” expresses a life experience: it has not happened at any point in his life.

videoQuiz1.text:
Look at the sentences:
Josh has not asked me out.   I have never asked a guy before.   You have never asked a guy before?
What verb is the main verb in the sentences?
videoQuiz1.explanation: In Present Perfect we use verbs in the third form (past participle). Regular verbs follow the “-ed” rule: ask → asked, promise → promised. Irregular verbs change their form: think → thought, tell → told.

videoQuiz1.text: What auxiliary verb comes before the main verb “asked”?
videoQuiz1.explanation: In Present Perfect the auxiliary verbs are have and has. They come before the main verb (past participle). “Do/does” belongs to Present Simple; “am/is/are” to Present Continuous; “did” to Past Simple.

videoQuiz1.text: What is the auxiliary verb when the subject is “John”?
videoQuiz1.explanation: “John” = he (3rd person singular) → has. We use “has” with He, She, It.

videoQuiz1.text: What is the auxiliary verb when the subjects are “I” and “You”?
videoQuiz1.explanation: In Present Perfect auxiliary verbs change according to the subject: I → have   You → have   He → has   She → has   It → has   We → have   They → have

videoQuiz1.text: Put the words in the correct order:
Put the words in the correct order:
videoQuiz1.explanation: Word order in Present Perfect: Subject + have/has + V3. I have called him today.

videoQuiz1.text: Put the words in the correct order:
videoQuiz1.explanation: With never the order is: Subject + have/has + never + V3. You have never visited Almaty.

videoQuiz1.text: Look at the sentence: John has not asked me out today.
What do we add to the auxiliary verb to make negative sentences?
videoQuiz1.explanation: We add not after the auxiliary verb: has not asked / have not seen. Contractions: hasn’t, haven’t.

videoQuiz1.text: Is the main verb the same in positive and negative sentences?
videoQuiz1.explanation: The main verb (past participle) stays the same. Only “not” is added after the auxiliary verb: has not asked.

videoQuiz1.text: Look at the sentence: I have never asked a guy out before.
Is this sentence positive or negative?
videoQuiz1.explanation: This sentence is negative — the word “never” makes it negative, even though there is no “not” in the sentence.

videoQuiz1.text: Is this a recent action with no specific time, or a life experience?
options: Recent action with no specific time
videoQuiz1.explanation: This is a life experience — “I have never asked a guy out before” refers to Rachel’s entire life up to now, not a specific recent event.

videoQuiz1.text: What word makes this sentence negative?
videoQuiz1.explanation:
We use “never” when we talk about life experiences to make negative statements instead of “not”.
Examples: I have never been to France.   He has never had a real job.   They have never tried Slavic cuisine.

videoQuiz1.text: Put the words in the correct order:
Put the words in the correct order:
videoQuiz1.correct: I have never climbed a mountain.
videoQuiz1.explanation: Negative life experience: Subject + have/has + never + V3. I have never climbed a mountain.

videoQuiz1.text: Put the words in the correct order:
videoQuiz1.explanation: With she: She + has + never + V3. She has never eaten sushi.

grammarBank1.title: Present Perfect +
grammarBank1.intro:
We use Present Perfect for events in the past but with present results; life experiences; recent events — but we don't say or ask exactly when they happened.

We often use the present perfect with ever (at any time in your life) and never (at no time in your life) when we talk about experiences.
grammarBank1.structure:
The word order for positive Present Perfect sentences is SAV3 (Subject + auxiliary verb + V3 / past participle).

In Present Perfect auxiliary verbs change according to the subjects:
Ihave asked
Youhave asked
Hehas asked
Shehas asked
Ithas asked
Wehave asked
Theyhave asked
examples: I have lost my keys — I can't open the door.

videoQuiz1b.text: Monica ___  Richard before she started dating Chandler.
Chandler ___  the name Richard many times recently.
Monica says: “I ___  his name only twice.”
Rachel and Ross ___  to Chandler's complaints.
The friends ___  on the orange couch for a long time.
Richard ___  a sensitive topic for Chandler.
videoQuiz1b.explanation: Richard = he (3rd person singular) → has become (irregular: become → become).

videoQuiz1b.text: You ___  the video and noticed Chandler's tie.
Joey ___  his book during the conversation.
videoQuiz1b.explanation: Joey = he (3rd person singular) → has read (irregular: read → read).

blocks.intro:
NEGATIVE
In negative present perfect sentences we use “hasn't”, “haven't”, or “never”.
The word order for negative sentences is SAV3 (Subject + auxiliary verb + not + V3 / past participle).
We use “never” when we talk about life experiences to make negative statements instead of “not”.

Contractions: haven't = have not    hasn't = has not
examples: I have never asked a guy out.

videoQuiz1d.text: Joshua ___  Rachel out yet, even though they spent an hour together.
Rachel says: “I ___  a guy out before.”
Joey and Phoebe ___  Rachel much with her problem yet.
Phoebe's experience ___  a secret; she's asked guys out thousands of times.
We ___  watching the scene yet, but it's already funny.

videoQuiz2.text:
Look at the sentences:
Have you ever had a dream of…?   Have you ever listened to your heart?

videoQuiz2.explanation: The word order in interrogative Present Perfect sentences is ASV3 (Auxiliary verb + Subject + V3 / past participle). The auxiliary verb have / has comes first.

videoQuiz2.text: Look at the sentence: Where have you been?

videoQuiz2.explanation: WH-question words (where, what, who, how, when) come before auxiliary verbs in interrogative sentences.
WH-question words come before auxiliary verbs in interrogative sentences.

videoQuiz2.text: Put the words in the correct order:
Put the words in the correct order:
videoQuiz2.correct: Have you ever dreamt of a holiday?
videoQuiz2.explanation: Word order: Have + you + ever + V3. Have you ever dreamt of a holiday?

videoQuiz2.text: Put the words in the correct order:
videoQuiz2.correct: Have you ever listened to your heart?
videoQuiz2.explanation: Word order: Have + you + ever + V3. Have you ever listened to your heart?

blocks.intro:
INTERROGATIVE
The word order in interrogative Present Perfect sentences is ASV3 (Auxiliary verb + Subject + V3 / past participle).
WH-question words come before auxiliary verbs in interrogative sentences.
examples: Have you ever read a Russian novel?

videoQuiz2b.explanation: “You” → Have you ever listened. Use “have” with I, you, we, they; the verb must be in past participle form.
Dora = she (3rd person singular) → Has Dora shown. Use “has” with he, she, it; “shown” is the past participle of “show”.

videoQuiz2b.text: ___ the doctors ___  this tool to hear the heart?
videoQuiz2b.explanation: “The doctors” = they (plural) → Have the doctors used. Use “have” with plural subjects; past participle of “use” is “used”.
“It” (3rd person singular) → Has it become. “Become” is an irregular verb: become → become (past participle stays the same).

videoQuiz2b.text: ___ we ___  how to name this medical instrument?
videoQuiz2b.explanation: “We” → Have we learned. Use “have” with we; past participle of “learn” is “learned”.

videoQuiz3.text:
Look at the sentences:
Edmund has got the medal.   Richard has got the medal.   George has got the medal.
When did Edmund, Richard, and George get their medals?
videoQuiz3.explanation: Present Perfect is used for recent actions whose result is visible or relevant right now.

videoQuiz3.text: Are they wearing the medals now?
videoQuiz3.explanation: Yes — the medals are on them now. The past action (getting the medal) has a clear present result.

videoQuiz3.text: Do we know exactly when Edmund, Richard, and George got their medals?
options: Yes, the exact time is clear.
Yes, it is happening right now.
videoQuiz3.explanation: In Present Perfect the exact time is not mentioned or important. The focus is on the result, not when it happened.

videoQuiz3.text: What do all three sentences show?
options: They got medals in the past and they have medals now.
They got medals in the past but we can't see the medals now.
videoQuiz3.explanation: Present Perfect connects a past action with its present result: they got medals → they have medals now.

videoQuiz3.text: What is the main focus of these sentences?
options: The exact time when they got medals
The result — they have medals now
videoQuiz3.explanation: Present Perfect focuses on the present result of a past action, not on when exactly it happened.

blocks.intro:
PRESENT RESULT
We use Present Perfect for recent actions and for actions that happened in the past but have results in the present.
examples: I have lost my keys — I can't open the door.
Edmund has got the medal — he has it now.
Edmund hasn't got the medal — now he is upset.

videoQuiz3b.text: Edmund looks very happy! He ________ a medal.
videoQuiz3b.explanation: Edmund = he (3rd person singular) → has got. Use “has” with he, she, it.

videoQuiz3b.text: Richard ________ his homework yet, so he can't go out to play.
videoQuiz3b.explanation: Richard = he (3rd person singular), negative → hasn't finished. Use “hasn't” (= has not) with he, she, it + past participle.

videoQuiz3b.text: ________ George ________ his dinner yet? He must be hungry.
videoQuiz3b.explanation: George = he (3rd person singular), question → Has George finished. Use “has” in questions with he, she, it + past participle.

videoQuiz3b.text: Be careful! Peppa ________ some water on the floor. It's still wet.
videoQuiz3b.explanation: Peppa = she (3rd person singular) → has spilled. The wet floor is the present result of the past action.

videoQuiz3b.text: Edmund and Richard are exhausted because they ________ the whole day.
videoQuiz3b.explanation: “Edmund and Richard” = they (plural) → have played. Use “have” with plural subjects.

videoQuiz3b.text: “Can I speak to George?” — “I'm sorry, he ________ to bed.”
videoQuiz3b.explanation: He (3rd person singular) → has gone. “Go” is an irregular verb: go → gone (past participle).

usage: Действие завершено, но результат важен сейчас
Период времени все еще не закончился

examples.dialogue: "I have come to ask a favor."

examples.translation: Я никогда раньше не путешествовал первым классом.

exercises.question: Выберите правильное предложение:
exercises.explanation: Present Perfect: have/has + Past Participle. "have been" - правильная форма.

exercises.question: Дополните: "She __ finished her project."
exercises.explanation: С "she" (третье лицо) используется "has" в Present Perfect.

exercises.question: Какой вариант верен?
options: They have go to the store.
They have been to the store.
They has been to the store.
They are go to the store.

exercises.question: Выберите отрицание в Present Perfect:
options: I not have seen this movie.

exercises.question: Какой вопрос грамматически верен?
options: Have you ever traveled to Asia?
Has you ever traveled to Asia?
Do you have traveled to Asia?

exercises.question: Завершите вопрос: "How many times __ she __ here?"
Выберите правильный вариант:
options: We haven't finish the work yet.
We haven't finished the work yet.
We haven't been finishing the work yet.
We has not finished the work yet.

exercises.question: Дополните: "They __ lived in this city for 10 years."
Найдите предложение, в котором используется Present Perfect:
options: I went to Paris last year.
I have been to Paris before.
I go to Paris every summer.
I am going to Paris tomorrow.
exercises.explanation: "Have been" - это Present Perfect, обозначающий жизненный опыт.

exercises.question: Какое из этих предложений содержит ошибку?
options: I have lived here for 5 years.
He have studied English since childhood.

exercises.question: Выберите правильное отрицание:
options: I have not been to America.
exercises.explanation: Оба варианта - have not been / haven't been - верны.

exercises.question: Дополните вопрос: "Have they __ the presentation?"
exercises.explanation: Present Perfect: Have + they + finished (Past Participle)?

exercises.question: Какое предложение выражает жизненный опыт?
options: I am eating sushi right now.
I have eaten sushi many times.
exercises.explanation: "Have eaten many times" - это Present Perfect, обозначающий жизненный опыт.

exercises.question: Выберите вопрос, требующий ответа "Yes, I have":
exercises.explanation: Present Perfect вопрос "Have you visited...?" = "Yes, I have."
